input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the MABI MAX 100 serial robot with six axes, designed for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining. Its main components include a permanent magnet synchronous motor, gear transmission system, and Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, which collectively enable precise motion control and high repeatability required for industrial applications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including real-time visualization via a dashboard interface, and the capability to identify both white-box and black-box friction models dynamically. These services enhance the accuracy of robot control systems by enabling precise friction compensation and predictive maintenance, directly supporting operational efficiency and precision in manufacturing processes.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for system abstraction. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs model component and connector structures. Simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and programming languages (C++, Java) are employed for detailed behavior specification and integration of heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs model component & connector perspectives for functional and logical architecture, including interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral aspects are described using MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines. Methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT handle complexity through abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical layers), requiring integration of heterogeneous tools and stakeholders for comprehensive MBSE descriptions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including SysML, RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT, structured across requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral aspects are captured via SysML state machines, MontiArc Automaton, and MathWorks Simulink, while analytical models integrate simulation tools and code in languages like C++ and Java. Data sources derive from physical system states through automated data flows, with data management addressing heterogeneous stakeholder integration across diverse modeling tools and notations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components consist of a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, with a gear transmission system comprising a primary gear followed by a cycloidal gear. Control inputs are managed through the motor current (IM), which serves as the primary electrical input for regulating actuation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive: a motor encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. Sensor data, specifically input current and output angle measurements, are streamed in real-time via MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical to virtual components via MQTT protocol, transmitting sensor time series data including input current and output angle as part of a one-way automated data flow as defined for digital shadows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for streaming sensor time series data between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system complies with industry standards including SysML [17], AADL [24], ACME [23], MontiArc [25, 26], and methodologies such as RFLP [18, 19], OOSEM [20], SPES [21], and SMArDT [22]. These standards support interoperability and integration of heterogeneous models and tools (e.g., Simulink, C++, Java) while addressing diverse stakeholder perspectives [31].

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the MABI MAX 100 robot, its configuration (six axes), its application (machining), and specific components (permanent magnet synchronous motor, gear transmission, Siemens controller) as described in the source. It is highly specific and grounded in the text.

The extraction correctly identifies real-time visualization via a dashboard and the capability to identify white-box and black-box friction models. However, it mentions 'predictive maintenance,' which is not explicitly stated in the provided text; the text focuses on 'enhance[ing] the robot’s motion accuracy' and dealing with 'wear' rather than explicitly defining a predictive maintenance service.

The extraction accurately lists the MBSE methodologies, ADLs, and simulation tools described in Section II.A. Supported by quotes: 'methodologies... e.g., RFLP... OOSEM... SPES... or SMArDT', 'architecture description languages (ADLs) are used... e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs', 'MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.
